title: Industrial Design Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541420 ($2,501,498,861) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541420 ($2,906,073,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $233,495 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541420); class <5: 1,208 firms, avg revenue/firm $356,066 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $55,724 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 177 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,730,514 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $270,825 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 94 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,101,245 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $485,345 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 85 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,402,424 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,627,979 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,169,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,068,948 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,040,567 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,414,849 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 131
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

current_adoption_pct.source: Autodesk 2024 State of Design & Make Report (5,399 industry leaders across AEC, design/manufacturing, media/entertainment); AI in Design Report 2026 (900+ designers at startups, enterprises, agencies)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Autodesk 2024: 56% of companies approaching/achieving AI goals across design & make industries. AI in Design 2026: 54% of designers used AI weekly in 2025 (tech-company bias; conservative downward adjustment applied for industrial design consultancies). ESTIMATE for material use at NAICS 541420 firms: ~25-35%, using 30% as midpoint. Material = AI integrated into CAD workflows, rendering, documentation or project management functions.

historical_analogs.value: CAD adoption in industrial/product design: AutoCAD/SolidWorks from early 1990s, ~50% of professional product design firms by early 2000s (~8-10 years). AI adoption pace estimated 2.34x faster than classical technology benchmarks (Dan Cumberland Labs 2026). From current baseline (~30% in 2026), S-curve deceleration to 50% in approximately 3-5 years. Using 4.0 years as central estimate.
historical_analogs.source: BIM adoption timeline: PlanRadar AEC survey 2021 (52% small firm BIM adoption by 2021 from ~2000 baseline = ~19 years); AI pace multiplier: Dan Cumberland Labs AI Adoption Curve AEC 2025; Autodesk 2024/2025 State of Design & Make
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: AI adoption is diffusing 2.34x faster than classical technology benchmarks; only 27% of AEC firms deploy AI for automation, problem-solving or decision-making (meaningful use vs nominal). 40% of professionals report companies approaching/achieving AI goals (Autodesk 2025).

owners_60plus_pct.source: ESTIMATE — no IDSA-specific owner age data found; cross-referenced general US small business owner demographics and architecture/design firm succession trends
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people 55+ (Project Equity 2024); architecture firm demographic wave: Baby Boomer founders approaching retirement (Architecture Paper 2026). Industrial design studio founders are predominantly Baby Boomer/older Gen-X consistent with the broader design sector trend. 60+ fraction estimated at 30%, consistent with design-sector demographics.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No IDSA (Industrial Designers Society of America) succession planning research found analogous to ASID's 2025 'Designing A Succession Plan' report. While general small business succession planning gaps are documented (54% of small businesses lack succession plans, Project Equity 2024), no industrial-design-specific evidence of a documented shortage is available. ASID data covers interior design (541410), not this code.

active_consolidators.source: ESTIMATE — no PE-backed platform identified doing systematic add-on acquisitions in NAICS 541420 in last 24 months. Historic buyers are consulting/tech strategics (McKinsey acquired Lunar Design, Accenture acquired Fjord) doing acqui-hire transactions, not PE roll-up platforms.
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Lunar bought by McKinsey, Fjord acquired by Accenture Interactive (2013), frog part of Capgemini Invent — all strategic/consulting acquisitions for capability, not PE roll-up EBITDA arbitrage. No PE-backed industrial design consolidation platform identified in 2024-2025.

buy_mult.source: ESTIMATE — no industrial design firm transaction database found with stated EBITDA multiples. Cross-referenced professional services small firm multiples: accounting/consulting practices at 3.0x-5.0x EBITDA; engineering/design firms (ROG Partners/Zweig, lower quartile 3.35x). Industrial design firms at $1-5M EBITDA are founder-dependent creative businesses; comparable to other founder-led professional services at small scale.
buy_mult.figure_quoted: A/E firm transaction study lower quartile 3.35x EBITDA (ROG Partners, 257 transactions). Small professional services/consulting firms: 3.0x-5.0x EBITDA range (Raincatcher, Axial forum). Industrial design at small scale estimated at 3.50x midpoint, discounted for founder-dependence and no PE roll-up buyer universe.

exit_mult.source: ESTIMATE — no documented industrial design platform exit on EBITDA basis. Cross-reference: A/E firm transactions median 5.30x (ROG Partners 2025); professional services consulting at mid-market 5x-7x. Strategic acquirers of design studios (consulting, tech) historically paid acqui-hire premiums not EBITDA-based; absent a PE platform exit comp, using A/E lower-median as ceiling for a hypothetical institutional exit.
exit_mult.figure_quoted: A/E firm transaction study median 5.30x, mean 5.91x EBITDA (ROG Partners 2025, 257 transactions). No industrial design platform exit documented. Using 5.50x as conservative ceiling, noting that strategic acquirers may pay differently from PE exit assumptions.

pricing_structure.value: Fixed-fee project engagements (~50-60% of revenue); hourly/time-and-materials (~30-40%); retainer for ongoing product development (~5-10%). Corporate clients with procurement departments re-scope and rebid at contract renewal (typically 12-24 month cycle), meaning AI efficiency gains on fixed-fee projects get competed away at renewal. Hourly billings directly lose revenue as AI cuts design hours.
pricing_structure.source: Design1st.com product design pricing guide; NMSConsulting.com consulting fee models 2026; Toggl consulting pricing models 2026
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Three primary models: flat fee (best for defined scopes), fee-for-service hourly ($100-$450/hr range), fee plus equity/royalties (niche). Most engagements are milestone-based fixed fee or hourly. No authoritative revenue-split survey found; 55/35/10 allocation is ESTIMATE based on trade coverage of product design consultancy billing practice.

breakdown_by_role.role: Interior Designers (27-1025) — maps to Industrial/Product Designers at 541420
breakdown_by_role.rationale: At 541420 the 27-1025 OEWS slot maps to the core industrial/product design occupation (27-1021 Commercial & Industrial Designers is absent from the 4-digit top-10 per prompt). CAD surfacing iterations, rendering, CMF documentation, and presentation materials are heavily AI-exposed (generative CAD and Autodesk AI capabilities now in commercial availability). Physical prototyping, field user research, DFM judgment with manufacturers, and client workshops are not automatable. 40% automatable (below 541410 interior designers at 45% due to more engineering/physical prototyping content in industrial design).

breakdown_by_role.role: Graphic Designers (27-1024) — maps to visualization/rendering/packaging staff
breakdown_by_role.rationale: At 541420 this slot represents the visualization, rendering, packaging graphics, and pitch-visual production staff in product studios. These are near-fully automatable production tasks (generative AI image, 3D rendering, layout). 25% retained for creative direction judgment and complex client-specific brand work.

Corporate client relationships and creative/studio direction persist as human work. Studio operations, financial reporting, internal scheduling, and administrative oversight automate moderately. 30% automatable captures ops/admin while preserving client-leadership and strategic direction functions.

Proposal deck generation, case-study content creation, and CRM pipeline management automate substantially. Enterprise relationship selling with corporate design procurement teams, conference presence, and referral cultivation persist. 40% automatable.

Schedule tracking, budget reporting, milestone documentation, and status communication automate substantially. Physical coordination with prototyping vendors, tooling shops, and client QA reviews require human presence and judgment. 45% automatable (slightly lower than interior design PM at 50% due to heavier physical/vendor-coordination content in industrial design).
General studio administration, correspondence, invoicing, filing — near-fully automatable. 20% retained for physical tasks and context-dependent judgment.
Shop support, model handling, material movement for physical prototyping — almost entirely physical, not automatable. Tiny 5% for receiving/inventory documentation.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Marketing content, case study creation, competitive analysis reports automate substantially. Field user research for product development (ethnography, usability sessions) and client-specific market intelligence require human judgment. 50% automatable.
Scheduling, correspondence, document management — near-fully automatable.

Creative direction for product aesthetics, CMF programs, and brand expression. Production rendering/visualization automates; strategic aesthetic direction and creative vision for corporate client products retains human judgment. 40% automatable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unallocated tail (all occupations not in top-10)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Residual 37.39% of wage bill. At 541420 this tail includes mechanical engineers and model-makers (physical prototyping — not automatable), engineering/CAD technicians (drawing packages, tolerance documentation — automatable), and bookkeeping/admin (automatable). Estimated 35% automatable — lower than interior design tail (38%) because the engineering/prototyping component is larger and less automatable in industrial design. Range reflects substantial uncertainty in true occupational mix.

pi_dist.rationale: Fixed-fee project engagements (~55% of revenue): short-term operator captures AI efficiency as margin, but corporate procurement departments re-scope and rebid at contract renewal (12-24 month cycle) — AI savings compete away over 1-2 cycles. Hourly billings (~35%): AI cuts hours directly → revenue falls (efficiency transfers to client). Retainer (~10%): operator captures efficiency. Blended effective pi_dist considering 1-2 year repricing lag: 0.55×0.65 + 0.35×0.25 + 0.10×0.80 = 0.3575 + 0.0875 + 0.08 = 0.525. Discounted further to 0.45 for founder-/studio-dependence: corporate clients increasingly bring design in-house, reducing leverage in renewal negotiations, and the studio brand is not transferable post-acquisition.
sources: Design1st.com — product design firm pricing models

pi_moat.rationale: Barrier strength is weak. No licensure or certification requirement for industrial design practice — any entity can offer 'design services'. No proprietary data, no technology lock-in (clients can switch tools/firms freely). Studio reputation and creative track record provide a soft moat but are founder-dependent and often non-transferable. Physical prototyping capability (model shop, 3D printing infrastructure, vendor network for tooling) provides a modest real barrier, but mid-size corporate clients increasingly maintain in-house prototyping or use on-demand services. The dominant addressable market risk is corporate insourcing — AI-enabled in-house teams can now substitute for more outsourced work. No gate between clients and generative design tools except creative specialization and physical testing capability.
sources: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook — Industrial Designers (no licensure requirement noted)
Medium/Teaque Lenahan on integrating design firms into consultancies (founder-dependence analysis)

t50_years.derivation: Current material AI adoption at ~25-35% (Autodesk 2024 State of Design & Make: 56% of design/make companies approaching/achieving AI goals broadly; conservative downward adjustment for industrial design consultancies yields ~30% material adoption in 2026). Historical analog: CAD adoption in product design reached ~50% in 8-10 years from early 1990s; AI diffusing 2.34x faster → effective analog ~3-4 years from current base. S-curve deceleration from current ~30% to 50% at slowing ~5pp/year → ~4 years to reach 50% from 2026. Central estimate: 4.0 years (landing ~mid-2030).

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Uplift = V_raw × 100 = 39.87pp. A = 2.5 (< 5). The flag condition (uplift < 3pp while A >= 5) does not apply. The 39.87pp is an artifact of the implausibly high labor_share (0.9177), which the prompt explicitly warns may be a vintage-mismatch artifact. True expected EBITDA uplift at a normalized labor_share of ~0.55-0.65 would be approximately 24-28pp — still above 3pp. The arithmetic is internally consistent given the provided input; the implausible labor_share is flagged in reviewer_note rather than overriding the formula result.

terminal_value.justification: Industrial design services face compound pressure from two directions. First, AI (generative CAD, rendering, documentation) automates core design production tasks that historically justified studio fees. Second, corporate insourcing trend — a decade-long shift as large companies build internal design teams — is accelerating because AI-enabled in-house teams of 2-3 can now substitute for outsourcing many product design engagements. The studio's durable residue: physical prototyping and model-shop capability, field user research (ethnography, usability testing with real users), DFM iteration at manufacturing partners, and top-tier creative reputation at the brand level. Classified PRESSURED rather than MELTING because physical prototyping, field research, and specialized manufacturing-interface work cannot be fully automated and retain genuine paid demand — but the addressable outsourced market shrinks as AI lowers the threshold for insourcing. Demand persists but at a compressed addressable market size.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from the provided dataset labor_share (0.9177, used as given per Rule 6) multiplied by ai_replaceable_share — a judgment on task automability by occupation role, with reference to Autodesk AI capabilities, generative CAD development timelines, and occupational duty descriptions. A was derived from current_adoption_pct sourced from Autodesk's State of Design & Make surveys and historical CAD adoption timeline in product design. Neither V nor A referenced PE deal counts, M&A consolidation activity, or strategic acquirer behavior.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Industrial design consultancies primarily bill on fixed-fee project engagements (~55% of revenue) and hourly time-and-materials (~35%). For fixed-fee projects with corporate clients: AI efficiency accrues to studio in the near term (1-2 years), but corporate procurement departments re-scope and rebid at contract renewal, competing AI savings away over a 1-2 year repricing cycle. For hourly billings: AI directly reduces chargeable hours, deflating revenue immediately. Net: moderate-to-high deflation risk. This is captured in pi_dist = 0.45 (below the naive blended first-pass of 0.525, discounted for insourcing pressure and studio non-transferability) and pi_moat = 0.35 (no structural regulatory or proprietary-data barrier to repricing pressure or client insourcing).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=2.5: A=4.0; at t50=7.0: A=1.429. S ranges from ~3.88 (t50=2.5) to ~3.16 (t50=7.0). Moderate spread (~0.72) on S given 5th-root dampening.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.50-0.70 for a normalized estimate (vs 0.9177 provided). V_raw would be 0.217-0.304 for ars=0.434, V=8.70-10.0. S range: 3.34-3.46 (V already at ceiling of 10.0 for most of range, so impact limited above ~0.575 labor_share).
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: V=10.0 is already at maximum. If labor_share were lower (0.50), V_raw=0.217, V=8.70, S≈3.34. The implausible labor_share drives V to 10.0 ceiling; actual impact on S is bounded because V is already maxed above labor_share ~0.575.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 4.00-7.00x (no documented industrial design platform; strategic acquirer comps not EBITDA-based)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At exit=4.00: M=0.571, S≈2.53. At exit=7.00: M=4.000, S≈4.28. Largest single uncertainty driver for S (range ~1.75 spread). If no institutional PE exit exists, effective M may be near 0 if only strategic acqui-hire buyers exist.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: C could range from 1.0 (pi_dist=0.35, pi_moat=0.30) to 2.25 (pi_dist=0.50, pi_moat=0.45). S ranges from ~3.08 to ~3.76.
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: C=1.575 is near the center of this range. The low moat and pricing-deflation risk are the structural story for this code — uncertainty in both pi_dist and pi_moat together shifts S by ~0.68.

reviewer_note: CRITICAL DATA QUALITY ISSUE: labor_share = 0.9177 is implausibly high, as explicitly flagged in the prompt (likely a vintage-mismatch artifact: 2024 QCEW wages divided by 2022 SUSB receipts in a small, fast-changing code where receipts may be understated). The formula is applied mechanically per Rule 1 and Rule 6, yielding V=10.0 (maximum). A sanity check: if true labor_share were 0.55-0.65 (reasonable for a pure-service professional studio), V_raw would be 0.239-0.282 and V would be 9.56-10.0 — still at or near the V ceiling for ars=0.434. Thus while the data quality flag is real, V's ceiling behavior means it doesn't materially affect S in this case. ROLE MIX CONTAMINATION (SEVERE): The provided role_mix is 4-digit 5414 and its core industrial design occupation (27-1021 Commercial & Industrial Designers) is entirely absent from the top-10. Interior Designers (18.41% wage share) and Graphic Designers (15.38%) dominate — both belonging to sibling codes 541410 and 541430. The within_role_automatable_fraction was calibrated for what occupants at a true 541420 firm would actually do (industrial/product design tasks) rather than interior design. This is flagged as hypothesis in all relevant role breakdowns. STRUCTURAL NOTE: No PE roll-up exists for this code; the historic buyer universe consists of management consulting and tech strategy firms buying design capability (Accenture-Fjord, McKinsey-Lunar, Capgemini-frog) — these are talent/capability acquisitions at acqui-hire economics that may not follow standard EBITDA multiple logic. The exit_mult=5.50x assumption is aspirational and highly uncertain; if the only realistic buyers are strategic acqui-hirers, effective M may be substantially lower. S=3.46 reflects a code with maximum V (from labor_share artifact) but structurally weak capture (C=1.575) and limited multiple arbitrage opportunity (M=2.29), with moderate B from fragmentation offset by small n_band.
